evals.name: file-release-notes
evals.prompt:
Read test-inputs/release-notes.md (relative to this evals directory) and rewrite it heavily — strip the AI slop, follow technical writing style guidelines, and return both the rewritten doc (as a new file with .deslopped before the extension) and a short summary of what you changed.

For the specific facts that the rewrite needs (since the original is intentionally vague), use these realistic stand-ins:
- Run submission: p99 latency to AWS Batch compute environments dropped from 12s to 4s (issue #1842)
- Launchpad: the pipeline list now loads through a paginated query, ~3x faster for workspaces with more than 1000 pipelines (issue #1856)
- Data Explorer: bucket listing batches storage API calls instead of issuing them sequentially (issue #1871)
- Security: SAML 2.0 and OIDC single sign-on added; RBAC with 5 built-in roles plus custom roles; audit log writes to S3 with optional WORM retention
- Breaking: the deprecated `tw --token` flag is removed (set `TOWER_ACCESS_TOKEN` instead); the CLI default output format is now `table` (was JSON), set with `--output`
- Migration guide URL: https://docs.seqera.io/platform-cloud/migrate/24.1
evals.expected_output: A new file at test-inputs/release-notes.deslopped.md with the rewritten content, plus a change summary in chat.

evals.name: phrases-rule-family
evals.prompt:
Deslop this concept page — heavy rewrite. Return the rewritten text and a short change summary.

```
# Compute environments

Here's the thing: a compute environment is where your pipelines actually run. It's worth noting that this is one of the most important concepts to understand. In this section, we'll explore what compute environments are and why they matter.

Let's dive in. A compute environment defines the cloud or HPC backend, the credentials, and the work directory for a run. Keep in mind that you can have more than one.

Seqera Platform is a powerful, comprehensive, best-in-class solution that empowers teams to leverage cutting-edge automation. It serves as the cornerstone of a modern bioinformatics ecosystem and helps democratize access to scalable compute. This release is a game-changer that will fundamentally reshape how your team thinks about pipeline orchestration. Let's delve into the rich tapestry of new capabilities that streamline your workflows and supercharge productivity.

In order to configure a compute environment, you basically need to perform the configuration of your credentials first. Due to the fact that the platform supports a number of providers, experts argue that you should really just pick the one closest to your data. The implications are significant. Industry reports suggest that misconfigured credentials cause numerous failures, and so on. It is widely believed that doing the validation of your settings early helps.

The platform allows you to store credentials centrally, and this feature enables you to connect to cloud storage. The system gives you the ability to validate them before any run. Validation acts as a sanity check on the stored values, unset optional fields fall back to a dummy value, the platform only reads from buckets on the whitelist, and pipelines track the master branch of their repository by default. For more information, [click here](#).

In conclusion, compute environments are the foundation of running pipelines on Seqera Platform.
```

Facts to keep: a compute environment defines the execution backend (AWS Batch, Azure Batch, Google Cloud Batch, Kubernetes, or an HPC scheduler), the credentials used to access it, and the work directory for pipeline runs. A workspace can hold multiple compute environments. The release adds run-level cost reporting and a bulk relaunch action — use these instead of the hype. Configure credentials before the compute environment; supported providers are AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud. Validating credentials before a run surfaces permission errors at setup instead of at launch. Stored credentials connect Seqera Platform to cloud storage; validation checks the stored values before any run; unset optional fields fall back to placeholder values; Seqera Platform reads only from buckets on the storage allowlist; pipelines track the main branch of their repository by default. The destination of the link is the credentials reference page.
evals.expected_output: A Concept that opens with a direct definition. Phrase-level fixes across four families: (1) openers and meta — removes the throat-clearing opener ('Here's the thing:'), hedge/emphasis crutches ('It's worth noting that', 'Keep in mind that'), self-referential framing ('In this section, we'll explore'), pedagogical openers ('Let's dive in'), and the signposted 'In conclusion' paragraph. (2) marketing and jargon — cuts marketing adjectives (powerful, comprehensive, best-in-class, cutting-edge), replaces sales verbs (empowers->lets, leverage->use, democratize->give access to, streamline->simplify, supercharge->speed up), replaces business jargon (game-changer, ecosystem, cornerstone), replaces the 'serves as' dodge with 'is', deflates 'fundamentally reshape', removes AI vocabulary tells (delve, tapestry), and states the two real changes (cost reporting, bulk relaunch). (3) padding, adverbs, vague — replaces padding ('In order to'->'To', 'Due to the fact that'->'Because'), cuts filler adverbs (basically, really, just), collapses nominalizations ('perform the configuration of'->'configure', 'doing the validation of'->'validate'), removes the vague declarative ('The implications are significant'), cuts or names vague attributions ('experts argue', 'Industry reports suggest', 'It is widely believed'), replaces 'a number of providers' with the named list, removes 'and so on'; the unsourced 'numerous failures' claim is reworded concretely or dropped with the drop flagged (never-invent rule). (4) customer perspective, inclusive language, links — rewrites product-as-subject phrasing to direct second person ('The platform allows you to'->'You can', 'enables you to'->'you can', 'gives you the ability to'->'you can'), and rewrites — not deletes — the descriptive credential sentences, replacing non-inclusive/ableist terms (sanity check->check/verification, dummy value->placeholder value, whitelist->allowlist, master branch->main branch) while keeping them as conceptual statements (the supplied facts back each one, so dropping them is over-cutting, not the never-invent rule), and replaces 'click here' with descriptive link text naming the credentials reference. Change summary lists the categories with counts.

evals.name: structures-rule-family
evals.prompt:
Deslop this, paying attention to structural patterns, sentence joins, and punctuation. Return the rewritten text and a short change summary.

```
## Token handling.

This isn't a bug. It's a design decision. Not slow. Not broken. Just misunderstood.

Why does retry behavior matter? Because runs fail. It retries fast. It retries quietly. It retries every time. The result? Resilient, reliable, robust pipelines. That's it. That's the feature.

Despite these challenges, the scheduler continues to scale. From small scripts to enterprise empires, the run decides when to fail and the metrics tell us why. The first wall is quota. The second wall is networking. The third wall is logging. This is the observability gap, underscoring its role as a critical component. Like a conductor leading an orchestra, the platform orchestrates everything. AWS didn't solve this. Google didn't solve this. Stripe didn't solve this.

In this section, we'll cover three benefits. Every user always wants faster runs — nobody wants to wait.

- **Performance**: Runs start in under 30 seconds → on a warm environment.
- **Reliability**: Tasks retry automatically — yes, automatically — up to three times.
- **Security**: Credentials are encrypted at rest.

So performance is fast. Now reliability is high. But security is strong. As discussed in this section, the platform is fast, reliable, and secure. Fast runs save time. Quick runs save effort. Speedy runs help everyone.

The access token expires after an hour, so refresh it before a run; the schema is cached, which means changes need a restart. There's one rule: keep tokens short-lived.

Set these:

- the "--profile" flag
- region, work directory and max CPUs
- enable retries.
```

Facts: failed tasks retry automatically up to 3 times, with exponential backoff starting at 30 seconds. Runs fail when the account hits its vCPU quota; the platform checks the quota before submitting jobs and queues the run if the quota is exceeded. Keep the three bullet facts (start time, retries, encryption). Tokens expire after 1 hour; the schema cache requires a restart after changes. The flag is `--profile`.
evals.expected_output: Structure-level fixes across four families: (1) false drama — removes the binary contrast ('This isn't a bug. It's a design decision.'), the negative listing ('Not slow. Not broken.'), the dramatic fragmentation ('That's it. That's the feature.'), the self-posed rhetorical questions ('Why does retry behavior matter?', 'The result?'), the anaphora ('It retries fast. It retries quietly. It retries every time.'), and the tricolon ('resilient, reliable, robust'); states the actual retry behavior (3 automatic retries, exponential backoff from 30s) in plain sentences. (2) formulas and agency — removes the 'Despite these challenges' dismissal, the false range ('From small scripts to enterprise empires'), the false agency ('the run decides', 'the metrics tell us' -> name the actor/process), the listicle-in-a-trench-coat ('The first wall... The second wall...' -> either a real list or, since only the vCPU-quota cause is in the supplied facts, dropping the unsupported networking/logging items per the never-invent rule with the drop flagged), the superficial participle ('underscoring its role as a critical component'), the invented concept label ('the observability gap'), the dead metaphor (conductor/orchestra), and the historical analogy stacking (AWS/Google/Stripe); states the real quota-check behavior. (3) markdown and rhythm — drops bold-first bullet leads where they don't carry information, removes decorative em-dashes ('— yes, automatically —') and the unicode arrow, cuts the fractal summary ('In this section, we'll cover' + 'As discussed in this section'), fixes paragraph-opener crutches ('So', 'Now', 'But'), replaces lazy extremes ('Every user always', 'nobody', 'everyone') with specifics, and collapses the one-point dilution ('Fast runs... Quick runs... Speedy runs...') into a single statement. (4) connectors and punctuation — splits the ', so' and ', which means' joins into separate sentences (or leads with the cause), replaces the semicolon and the mid-sentence colon ('one rule: keep...') with periods, removes the terminal period from the heading, adds the Oxford comma ('region, work directory, and max CPUs'), makes the list punctuation parallel (all-or-none periods), and puts the flag in backticks rather than quotes (`--profile`). Change summary lists the structural patterns removed.

evals.name: style-terminology-clarity-rule-family
evals.prompt:
Deslop this Seqera Platform task page, applying the house style guide, terminology rules, and clarity rules. This is current documentation (not legacy). Return the rewritten text and a short change summary.

```
# Configuring The Compute Environment

## **Step 1: Log In**

Utilize the form to log in, e.g. with your email. Since the token is missing, you can not launch a run. Set TOWER_ACCESS_TOKEN to authenticate. Click the `Launch` button to start.

The job is submitted to AWS Batch by Tower. A new access token will be generated and the migration should be run before the upgrade is performed. The new release is significantly faster and supports various providers, which should improve things for a number of users. Runs use HPC or GCP backends.

The platform stores your Credentials and Compute Environments per Workspace. The executor runs the NextFlow pipeline tasks automatically. When you configure a compute environment you need to ensure that the credentials you are using have the appropriate permissions for the cloud provider, which typically means access to create and manage instances, storage, and networking resources for the run. The executor and the scheduler then coordinate.

The workflow failed to launch from the Launch Pad. Open the data explorer, then check the executions in the runs tab and view the multiQC report. Set the --profile flag and open nextflow.config. Each job ran on its own node.

#### Notes

There are several project integration custom settings. It is recommended that it be configured early. When it fails, controlling the retry logic helps, but don't sweat it — it's not rocket science. You will utilize these settings later.

For testing, use token sk-1a2b3c-real-looking-secret and email admin@acme-corp.com.
```

Facts: this is a task page; the page title supplies the H1. The UI button label is **Launch**. The release reduces run-submission p99 latency from 12s to 4s and adds support for Google Cloud Batch (joining AWS Batch and Azure Batch). An executor is the system that runs pipeline tasks (for example AWS Batch or Kubernetes). The credentials need permission to create and manage instances, storage, and networking. The failed item is a Platform pipeline (a runnable Launchpad item), not Nextflow code. The reader is looking at a single pipeline execution made of units of work. The Notes section is reference material, so prefer long forms over contractions there. Treat the example token and email as fake info to replace.
evals.expected_output: Style, terminology, and clarity fixes across six families: (1) voice, tense, specificity — converts passive to active voice ('The job is submitted... by Tower' -> 'Seqera Platform submits the job...'; 'the migration should be run' -> 'Run the migration'), converts future to present tense ('will be generated' -> 'generates'), and replaces vague claims with specifics ('significantly faster' -> the 12s->4s p99 figure; 'various providers' / 'a number of users' -> the named providers). (2) word choice, headings, capitalization — applies the word-choice cheatsheet (utilize->use, log in->sign in, 'e.g.'->'for example', 'can not'->'cannot', click->select), fixes headings (sentence case, no bold in headings, removes the redundant H1 since the page title supplies it — the title rename 'Configuring The Compute Environment' -> 'Configure the compute environment' applies to the supplied page title — and fixes the skipped heading level H2->H4). (3) localization, contractions, fake info — removes subject-hiding constructions ('There are', 'It is recommended that'), replaces ambiguous 'it' with the actual noun, prefers a simple verb over the -ing form ('controlling the retry logic' -> 'control the retry logic'), cuts the culture-specific idiom ('not rocket science'), breaks up the noun string ('project integration custom settings' -> 'custom settings for project integrations'), uses long forms in the reference-style Notes (don't->do not, it's->it is), and replaces the real-looking token/email with a placeholder (`<your_access_token>`) and an example.com address. (4) product names and abbreviations — replaces 'Tower' with 'Seqera Platform' and 'the platform' (meaning the product) with 'Seqera Platform', corrects 'NextFlow'->'Nextflow' and 'multiQC'->'MultiQC', lowercases the feature nouns (credentials, compute environments, workspace), puts the environment variable in backticks (`TOWER_ACCESS_TOKEN`), and spells out abbreviations on first use (high-performance computing (HPC), Google Cloud Platform (GCP)). (5) clarity — defines 'executor' on first use (the system that runs pipeline tasks, e.g. AWS Batch or Kubernetes), splits the 40-plus-word sentence into shorter ones (most under ~25 words), and notes the recurring 'executor' as a glossary candidate. (6) precision and formatting — fixes pipeline vs workflow ('The workflow failed to launch' -> 'The pipeline failed to launch', since it is a Platform pipeline) and run vs task/job ('executions'/'job' -> run/task as appropriate), corrects UI names (Launch Pad -> **Launchpad**, data explorer -> **Data Explorer**, runs tab -> **Runs**), and applies bold-vs-backticks (UI elements bold: **Launch** button, not backticks; code/identifiers in backticks: `--profile`, `nextflow.config`). Change summary names the style, terminology, and clarity fix families with counts.

evals.name: topic-types-rule-family
evals.prompt:
Deslop and re-type this multi-section doc. It contains a mistyped overview, a settings reference, a troubleshooting section, and a walkthrough. Return the rewritten text and a short change summary.

```
# Overview

Workspaces organize your work. This overview will help you understand them.

You should make sure you have an organization first. Then, to create a workspace: 1) you navigate to the organization, 2) you should click New, 3) you can enter a name.

## Summary

That's how workspaces work!

# Settings

region is the AWS region. It's really important to choose this carefully because it affects latency.
workDir is the work directory.
maxCpus is the max CPUs. As mentioned, this matters for cost.

# Launch problems

Sometimes things go wrong when you launch. Don't worry! If you see an access denied message, it might be due to a variety of factors.

# Pipeline Walkthrough - A Complete And Exhaustive End To End Guide To Everything

In today's fast-paced world, let's embark on an exciting journey together that will transform how you work!

You need an account. First you set up a compute environment. Then you launch nf-core/rnaseq. Finally you review results.

## More info
See the other page.
```

Facts: a workspace groups pipelines, compute environments, and credentials for a team. Prerequisite: an organization. To create one: go to the organization, select **New**, enter a name, then select **Add**. Settings: region has no default; workDir has no default; maxCpus defaults to 256. The troubleshooting case: the error is `AccessDenied (Service: S3, Status Code: 403)`; cause is that the compute environment credentials lack write access to the work-directory bucket; resolution is to grant the credentials `s3:PutObject` on that bucket and relaunch. The tutorial sets up an AWS Batch compute environment, launches the nf-core/rnaseq pipeline, and reviews results in the **Runs** tab; prerequisites are a Seqera Platform account and workspace, AWS credentials, and an S3 bucket for the work directory; outcome is a completed rnaseq run.
evals.expected_output: Topic-type fixes across all five types: (1) Concept + Task — splits the mistyped overview into a Concept with a noun title ('Workspaces', not 'Overview') containing no steps, and a Task with an active verb + noun title ('Create a workspace'), a `Prerequisites:` list (an organization), imperative numbered steps ('Go to...', 'Select **New**...', not 'you should click'), and no 'Summary'/'Conclusion' section; UI labels bolded, click->select. (2) Reference — converts the settings rows into a table (Setting | Type | Default | Description) with the 256 default, removing the prose narrative between rows ('It's really important...', 'As mentioned...'). (3) Troubleshooting — moves the actual error into the title with an `Error:` prefix and backticks (`AccessDenied (Service: S3, Status Code: 403)`), drops the chatty opener ('Don't worry!'), names the single cause instead of hedging ('a variety of factors'), and structures the body as symptom -> cause -> numbered resolution using the term 'resolution' or 'resolve'. (4) Tutorial + titles — a `Tutorial:` prefix with an active verb, sentence case, under 70 characters, no hyphen-as-separator (e.g. 'Tutorial: Run nf-core/rnaseq on AWS Batch'), opens with a one-sentence goal/outcome, uses a 'Before you begin' prerequisites section, task-style active-verb section headings ('Set up the compute environment', 'Launch the pipeline', 'Review the run'), hype/journey framing removed, and the one-sentence 'More info' stub folded in or made a Related topics link rather than its own topic. Change summary names all topic types produced and the structural fixes, with counts.

evals.name: passive-mode-light-touch
evals.prompt:
Deslop this concept page in passive mode (light-touch — word- and sentence-level fixes only, no restructuring). Return the rewritten text and a change summary.

```
# Configuring Workspaces

In this section, we'll explore workspaces. Workspaces are a powerful, comprehensive feature that empowers your team to seamlessly organize their work.

It's worth noting that a workspace groups your pipelines, compute environments, and credentials. In order to create one, you basically just need to do the following: 1) navigate to your organization, 2) click the `New` button, 3) enter a name, and 4) click Add.

## Troubleshooting

If you see an access denied error, don't worry — it might be due to a variety of factors. You should verify that your credentials have the appropriate permissions.

In conclusion, workspaces are the foundation of your work in Seqera Platform.
```

Facts: a workspace groups pipelines, compute environments, and credentials for a team. To create one: go to the organization, select **New**, enter a name, then select **Add**. The troubleshooting case: the error is `AccessDenied (Service: S3, Status Code: 403)`; the cause is that the compute environment credentials lack write access to the work-directory bucket; the resolution is to grant the credentials `s3:PutObject` on that bucket and relaunch. This is current Seqera Platform documentation.
evals.expected_output: Mode line `Mode: passive` on the first line of the change summary. Word- and sentence-level fixes APPLIED in place: removes the self-referential opener ('In this section, we'll explore'), cuts the marketing adjectives (powerful, comprehensive) and 'seamlessly' and replaces the sales verb (empowers -> lets), removes the hedge ('It's worth noting that'), replaces padding ('In order to' -> 'To') and cuts the filler adverbs (basically, just), removes the chatty troubleshooting opener ('don't worry'), replaces the hedge 'it might be due to a variety of factors' with the named cause from the facts (the credentials lack write access to the work-directory bucket), converts 'You should verify' to the imperative 'Verify', removes the literal 'In conclusion' signpost phrase, and applies terminology/format fixes within the existing text (click -> select, `New` button -> **New**, **Add** bolded). Structural changes are NOT performed; instead they are FLAGGED under a `Recommend (full mode):` block: (1) rename the title 'Configuring Workspaces' to the noun 'Workspaces' (concept title rule); (2) split the embedded numbered steps into a separate Task with an active-verb title ('Create a workspace'); (3) move the inline `## Troubleshooting` section off the page to the product's troubleshooting pages with a pointer left behind, retitle it with an `Error:` prefix and the real message in backticks (`AccessDenied (Service: S3, Status Code: 403)`), and restructure the body as symptom -> cause -> resolution (the `docs-structure` skill handoff is recommend-only in passive mode); (4) cut the restatement paragraph that begins 'workspaces are the foundation...'. The doc is NOT split, the title is NOT renamed, the troubleshooting is NOT moved, and no headings are added or removed. Classification (Concept with embedded steps) is still stated.

evals.name: verbose-mode-before-after-summary
evals.prompt:
Deslop this task page in verbose mode (show each change as a before/after pair with the rule that triggered it). Return the rewritten text and a verbose change summary.

```
# Generating an access token

This guide explains how to generate an access token. The platform allows you to create tokens that empower your scripts to authenticate.

In order to create a token, simply utilize the Settings page. It's worth noting that the token is shown only once. The token will be generated instantly and you can not retrieve it later, so copy it immediately.

To create a token:
1. Click your avatar, then click Settings.
2. Click New token and enter a name.
3. Click Copy to copy the token value.
```

Facts: this is a task page. The UI labels are **Settings**, **New token**, and **Copy**. A personal access token (PAT) authenticates the `tw` CLI and API calls. The token is displayed once and cannot be retrieved later. This is current Seqera Platform documentation.
evals.expected_output: Mode line `Mode: verbose` followed by the classification line `Classified as **Task**`. The rewrite itself is a normal full-mode Task rewrite (verbose mode changes only Artifact 2, not what is edited). Instead of 3-7 category bullets, the summary lists each significant edit as a `before -> after` pair grouped by triggering rule/reference, for example: self-referential framing (`phrases.md`): 'This guide explains how to generate an access token.' -> removed; customer perspective / allow (`style-guide.md`): 'The platform allows you to create tokens' -> 'You can create tokens' (or 'Use the Settings page to create tokens'); sales verbs (`phrases.md`): 'empower your scripts to authenticate' -> 'your scripts use to authenticate'; padding (`phrases.md`): 'In order to' -> 'To'; adverbs / word choice (`style-guide.md`): 'simply' -> cut, 'utilize' -> 'use'; hedge (`phrases.md`): 'It's worth noting that' -> removed; future->present and passive->active (`style-guide.md`): 'The token will be generated instantly' -> 'Seqera Platform generates the token instantly'; word choice (`style-guide.md`): 'can not' -> 'cannot'; loose connector (`structures.md`): '...retrieve it later, so copy it immediately' -> '...cannot retrieve it later. Copy it immediately.'; title rename (`topic-types.md`, Task): 'Generating an access token' -> 'Generate an access token'; terminology (`terminology.md`): UI labels bolded (**Settings**, **New token**, **Copy**), `tw` in backticks, and 'personal access token (PAT)' spelled out on first use. Trivial repeated swaps are collapsed into a single entry with a count rather than listed individually: 'click -> select x4' (`style-guide.md`). The summary still reports any summary-only obligations (here, none dropped). The graded behavior is the FORMAT: per-edit before/after pairs grouped by rule, the mode line present, the classification kept, and repeated swaps counted -- not the scannable category bullets of the default summary.

evals.name: passive-plus-verbose-combined
evals.prompt:
Deslop this concept page in passive + verbose mode (light-touch fixes only, and show each change as a before/after pair). Return the rewritten text and the change summary.

```
# Configuring Workspaces

In this section, we'll explore workspaces. Workspaces are a powerful, comprehensive feature that empowers your team to seamlessly organize their work.

It's worth noting that a workspace groups your pipelines, compute environments, and credentials. In order to create one, you basically just need to do the following: 1) navigate to your organization, 2) click the `New` button, 3) enter a name, and 4) click Add.

## Troubleshooting

If you see an access denied error, don't worry — it might be due to a variety of factors. You should verify that your credentials have the appropriate permissions.

In conclusion, workspaces are the foundation of your work in Seqera Platform.
```

Facts: a workspace groups pipelines, compute environments, and credentials for a team. To create one: go to the organization, select **New**, enter a name, then select **Add**. The troubleshooting case: the error is `AccessDenied (Service: S3, Status Code: 403)`; the cause is that the compute environment credentials lack write access to the work-directory bucket; the resolution is to grant the credentials `s3:PutObject` on that bucket and relaunch. This is current Seqera Platform documentation.
evals.expected_output: Mode line `Mode: passive + verbose`. The rewrite is the LIGHT-TOUCH (passive) rewrite: only word- and sentence-level fixes are applied in place, with no topic split, no title rename, no troubleshooting move, and no headings added or removed -- the same applied/suppressed split as the passive eval (id 6). Artifact 2 uses the VERBOSE format: each applied edit is shown as a `before -> after` pair grouped by triggering rule (self-referential opener removed; 'powerful'/'comprehensive' cut; 'empowers' -> 'lets'; 'seamlessly' cut; 'It's worth noting that' removed; 'In order to' -> 'To'; 'basically'/'just' cut; 'don't worry' removed; 'a variety of factors' -> the named cause; 'You should verify' -> 'Verify'; click -> select; `New` -> **New**), and the suppressed structural changes are listed under a `Recommend (full mode):` block (rename title to 'Workspaces'; split the steps into a 'Create a workspace' Task; move the inline troubleshooting off-page with an `Error:` title and a symptom -> cause -> resolution body; cut the 'workspaces are the foundation...' restatement paragraph). This verifies the two modes compose: passive governs WHAT is edited and verbose governs HOW the summary is formatted, and the recommend block still appears, rendered in the verbose style.
